task: Check the /migrations page on production: does it show real data with actual numbers? Are the editorial insights rendering? Is the stats bar showing live counts? Quick UX check — not a code review, just verify the user experience is working.

assignments.frontend: Check the /migrations page on production (indiestack.ai/migrations). Verify: 1) The page loads successfully with real data — actual tool names and migration numbers, not placeholder/empty content. 2) Editorial insights are rendering — look for descriptive text sections that explain migration trends. 3) The stats bar at the top shows live counts with actual numbers (not zeros or loading states). 4) General UX check — layout looks right, nothing broken or misaligned. Use curl to fetch the page HTML and inspect the rendered content. Report what's working and what's not.

decomposition.reasoning: This is a pure UX verification task — checking that a production page displays real data correctly. No code changes, no backend logic review, no deploy needed. Frontend department can fetch the production page and verify the rendered HTML contains real data, editorial insights, and live stats. Single department, single pass.

strategy_review.reasoning: Low-cost UX verification on a live production page. IndieStack uses server-side f-string templates (no JS rendering), so curl will show the actual content users see. If /migrations is live but broken or showing empty data, that's a credibility hit with every visitor and any AI agent that links to it. Past simple verification tasks (health check, landing page audit) cost $0.05-0.30 — this should be similar. Good hygiene at minimal cost.

approved_tasks.frontend: Check the /migrations page on production (indiestack.ai/migrations). Verify: 1) The page loads successfully with real data — actual tool names and migration numbers, not placeholder/empty content. 2) Editorial insights are rendering — look for descriptive text sections that explain migration trends. 3) The stats bar at the top shows live counts with actual numbers (not zeros or loading states). 4) General UX check — layout looks right, nothing broken or misaligned. Use curl to fetch the page HTML and inspect the rendered content. Report what's working and what's not.

conditions: Report only — no code changes. If something is broken, describe the problem so Patrick can decide whether to fix it.
If the page 404s, just say so and stop — don't go investigating route files or trying to fix it.
